coredata.dc:title: Low-Pt coupled with atomic Ni-N5 doped carbon dots as efficient ORR/HER bifunctional catalyst
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yuemei
Ma, Junhong
Jia, Shaorui
Ma, Chaoyun
Wu, Peiran
Yang, Lijing
Shen, Liuliu
Zhang, Guirong
coredata.dc:description: Constructing supported catalysts is an effective means to address the critical yet challenging issue of reducing platinum (Pt) usage while remaining its catalytic activity and stability. The marked effect of the support's physicochemical properties on the catalytic activity of the loaded Pt mandates an elaborate design and regulation to carbon carriers. Herein, we provide a simple and eco-friendly approach to prepare carbon dots support material with plenty of defect structure and atomically dispersed Ni-N5 sites (Ni-NC), using non-toxic chitosan as precursor. This material was further utilized to anchor Pt to achieve NiPt-NC electrocatalyst with highly dispersed Pt nanoparticles (NPs) (∼2 nm) and low Pt loading (2.49 wt%). The Experimental and theoretical calculation results illustrate that the interaction between Pt NPs and surrounding Ni-N5 sites leads to the electron enrichment around Pt, which not only optimizes the desorption of ∗OH and adsorption/dissociation of H2O (Volmer step), therefore improving the intrinsic activity of Pt for both oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), but also effectively enhances its catalytic stability. Especially, in practical rechargeable Zn-air batteries (RZABs) tests, NiPt-NC acheives a power density of 176 mW cm- 2 and sustains a cycling stability of 160 h. Furthermore, a water electrolysis cell assembled with NiPt-NC displays a current density of 10 mA cm- 2 at a voltage of merely 1.50 V, and maintains a current density of 20 mA cm- 2 without significant degradation for 30 h. The superior catalytic properties of NiPt-NC underscore its promising potential for application in practical devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Low Pt loading
Metal-support interaction
Atomic Ni-N5 sites
Oxygen reduction reaction
Hydrogen evolution reaction

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst misctext orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Shanghai cooperation organization science and technology partnership project

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Electrochemical hydrogen-water conversion (H2 + O2 \u21C4 H2O) represents a clean and efficient solution for the implementation of a sustainable energy system. However, the energy conversion efficiency of this energy system is limited by the sluggish kinetics of two key electrochemical reactions, namely, oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) [1,2]. Therefore, developing effective and durable catalysts for both ORR and HER are of great significance. At present, platinum (Pt) is generally acknowledged as the most efficient catalysts for both ORR and HER, but the scarcity and high cost of Pt astrict the large-scale application of Pt-based catalyst. Therefore, how to improve the utilization and catalytic efficiency of Pt catalysts and thus reducing Pt usage is a hotspot of current research [3]. Many researches have shown that when constructing Pt-based alloy catalyst using first-row transition metal (M) with smaller atomic radius, such as Fe [4], Co [5], and Ni [6], the electron and stress relationships between Pt-M can be optimized, which would improve the intrinsic activity of Pt [7]. In addition, downsizing of Pt nanoparticles (Pt NPs) to the nanoscale, or even to the single atom state, is an effective way to improve Pt utilization efficiency. Meanwhile, the quantum-size effects and unsaturated coordination environments generated by small-sized Pt NPs usually yield unexpected intrinsic activities [8,9]. However, the high surface energy of the small-sized Pt NPs makes them prone to aggregation, resulting in poor catalytic stability. At present, immobilizing Pt NPs onto specific support materials is recognized as an effective strategy to solve this problem, the metal-support interaction (MSI) not only stabilizes and disperses Pt, but also alters its electronic structure and thus enhancing its catalytic performance [10,11].This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Grant No. 21862021]; and the Shanghai cooperation organization science and technology partnership project [Grant No.2021E01009]. The authors thank the staff at BL17B1beamline of the National Facility for Protein Science in Shanghai (NFPS), Shanghai Advanced Research Institute, CAS, for providing technical support in XAFS data collection and analysis.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Grant No. 21862021]; and the Shanghai cooperation organization science and technology partnership project [Grant No.2021E01009].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram depicting the process for obtaining NiPt-NC; (b) FTIR spectra, (c) XRD pattern, and (d) Raman spectra of representative samples.
N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms, and the corresponding pore size distributions for the NC, Ni-NC, Pt-NC, and NiPt-NC

HR-TEM images of (a) NC, (b) Ni-NC, (c) Pt-NC, and (d) NiPt-NC; (e-h) AC-HAADF-STEM image and the corresponding elemental mapping of NiPt-NC; (i-k) Line-scanning intensity profiles and EELS spectra obtained from the red dotted circle in Fig. 3e; (l) EELS spectra obtained from the blue dotted circle in Fig. 3e. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) N 1s XPS spectra, (b) Ni 2p XPS spectra, (c) Pt 4f XPS spectra, (d) Ni K-edge XANES spectra, (e) FT-EXAFS spectra, (f) WT-EXAFS spectra, (g) Pt L3-edge XANES spectra, (h) FT-EXAFS spectra, and (i) WT-EXAFS spectra of different samples.

(a) ORR LSV curves; (b) Statistical histograms of E1/2 and MA of Pt-NC, NiPt-NC, and Pt/C; (c) Tafel plots extrapolated from the LSV curves; (d) LSV curves at different rotation rates and the corresponding K-L plots of NiPt-NC; (e) HO2
- yields and electron transfer numbers of NiPt-NC; (f) Stability for NiPt-NC and Pt/C.

Performance of RZABs using NiPt-NC + RuO2 or Pt/C+RuO2 as air cathode: (a) schematic representation of the RZABs; (b) Open-circuit potential test; (c) discharging polarization curves and power density curves; (d) Chronopotentiometric response at different current densities; (e) specific capacity, and (f) long-term cycling performance.

(a) HER LSV curves and corresponding (b) Tafel slope of different samples; (c) Overpotential and MA of Pt-NC, NiPt-NC, and Pt/C; (d) Double Layer Capacitor (Cdl); (e) LSV curves of two-electrode water splitting using NiPt-NC || RuO2 and Pt/C || RuO2 (insert is the digital photograph of the gas generated); (f) Stability of NiPt-NC || RuO2 and Pt/C || RuO2 toward overall water splitting.

(a and b) Free energy diagrams of the elementary steps in ORR and HER; (c and d) Schematic representation of the ORR and HER mechanisms on NiPt-NC(Grey, blue, red, pink, green, and magenta spheres represent carbon C, N, O, H, Ni, and Pt atoms, respectively); (e) The difference charge density analysis (The yellow and blue regions represent charge accumulation and depletion, respectively); (f) Calculated PDOS for Pt and Ni d orbital of Pt-NC and NiPt-NC surfaces. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Low-Pt coupled with atomic Ni-N5 doped carbon dots as efficient ORR/HER bifunctional catalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Xinjiang University, Urumqi, 830017, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources
School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Xinjiang University, Urumqi, 830017, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tiangong University, Tianjin, 300387, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tiangong University, Tianjin, 300387, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Constructing supported catalysts is an effective means to address the critical yet challenging issue of reducing platinum (Pt) usage while remaining its catalytic activity and stability. The marked effect of the support's physicochemical properties on the catalytic activity of the loaded Pt mandates an elaborate design and regulation to carbon carriers. Herein, we provide a simple and eco-friendly approach to prepare carbon dots support material with plenty of defect structure and atomically dispersed Ni-N5 sites (Ni-NC), using non-toxic chitosan as precursor. This material was further utilized to anchor Pt to achieve NiPt-NC electrocatalyst with highly dispersed Pt nanoparticles (NPs) (∼2 nm) and low Pt loading (2.49 wt%). The Experimental and theoretical calculation results illustrate that the interaction between Pt NPs and surrounding Ni-N5 sites leads to the electron enrichment around Pt, which not only optimizes the desorption of ∗OH and adsorption/dissociation of H2O (Volmer step), therefore improving the intrinsic activity of Pt for both oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), but also effectively enhances its catalytic stability. Especially, in practical rechargeable Zn-air batteries (RZABs) tests, NiPt-NC acheives a power density of 176 mW cm- 2 and sustains a cycling stability of 160 h. Furthermore, a water electrolysis cell assembled with NiPt-NC displays a current density of 10 mA cm- 2 at a voltage of merely 1.50 V, and maintains a current density of 20 mA cm- 2 without significant degradation for 30 h. The superior catalytic properties of NiPt-NC underscore its promising potential for application in practical devices.

Enhanced metal-support interactions between Low-loading platinum and Ni–N5 rich carbon lead to a bifunctional performance of HER and ORR.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical hydrogen-water conversion (H2 + O2 ⇄ H2O) represents a clean and efficient solution for the implementation of a sustainable energy system. However, the energy conversion efficiency of this energy system is limited by the sluggish kinetics of two key electrochemical reactions, namely, oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) [1,2]. Therefore, developing effective and durable catalysts for both ORR and HER are of great significance. At present, platinum (Pt) is generally acknowledged as the most efficient catalysts for both ORR and HER, but the scarcity and high cost of Pt astrict the large-scale application of Pt-based catalyst. Therefore, how to improve the utilization and catalytic efficiency of Pt catalysts and thus reducing Pt usage is a hotspot of current research [3]. Many researches have shown that when constructing Pt-based alloy catalyst using first-row transition metal (M) with smaller atomic radius, such as Fe [4], Co [5], and Ni [6], the electron and stress relationships between Pt-M can be optimized, which would improve the intrinsic activity of Pt [7]. In addition, downsizing of Pt nanoparticles (Pt NPs) to the nanoscale, or even to the single atom state, is an effective way to improve Pt utilization efficiency. Meanwhile, the quantum-size effects and unsaturated coordination environments generated by small-sized Pt NPs usually yield unexpected intrinsic activities [8,9]. However, the high surface energy of the small-sized Pt NPs makes them prone to aggregation, resulting in poor catalytic stability. At present, immobilizing Pt NPs onto specific support materials is recognized as an effective strategy to solve this problem, the metal-support interaction (MSI) not only stabilizes and disperses Pt, but also alters its electronic structure and thus enhancing its catalytic performance [10,11].

Carbon-supported Pt NPs (Pt/C) are classical Pt-based catalysts. Among them, the nature of the carbon-based support materials has crucial effects on the dispersion and electronic/geometric structure of the loaded Pt species. In recent years, nanomaterials comprised of N-coordinated metal single-atom embedded in carbon (M-NC) have gained recognition as promising supports for Pt catalysts [12–15]. This is attributed to the fact that M/N doping not only can modulate MSI, but also provides supplementary active sites such as M-N, Nx-C et al., which would boost the catalytic efficiency of Pt while reducing its required loading [16]. For instance, a carbon framework derived from MOF with Mn single-atom center (Mn-NC) has been used as a substrate to anchor Pt (1.98 wt%), the fine Pt NPs size and beneficial charge density redistribution between the Mn-N4 sites and Pt result in significant improvement in both ORR and HER performances [17]. Zeng et al. fabricated Pt atomic clusters riveted on carbon support containing Cr-N4 sites, which exhibits excellent HER behavior under alkaline conditions due to the Pt-Cr quasi-covalent bonding interaction [18]. Beyond the M-N sites in M-NC supports, the intrinsic attributes of the carbon support, including specific surface area, defectivity, and conductivity, also significantly modulate the dimensional and electronic configurations of Pt species [8,19,20]. Consequently, rational design of M-NC support structure is crucial for fully utilizing the MSIand obtaining efficient and cost-effective Pt based catalysts.

Herein, we designed and prepared a Ni and N co-doped carbon dot support material (Ni-NC) with abundant defective structure and atomically dispersed Ni-N5 sites, which was further loaded with low Pt (2.49 wt%) to obtain NiPt-NC catalyst. Thanks to the favorable Ni-NC structure enable a highly dispersed Pt NPs (∼2 nm) which effectively improve the utilization efficiency of Pt, the enhanced electronic effect between Pt and Ni-N5 that optimize the electronic structures of Pt and the adsorption behavior of the key intermediates, and multiple active sites including Ni-N5, Nx-C, and carbon defects et al. coexist in the NiPt-NC, the achieved NiPt-NC exhibits superior catalytic activity and long-term stability for both ORR and HER. More importantly, the practical rechargeable Zn-air batteries (RZABs) and water electrolyzer based on NiPt-NC exhibit excellent performance and stability.

Synthesis of Ni-NC and NC: Typically, chitosan (1.0 g), citric acid (1.0 g), l-Arginine (0.3 g) and NiCl2·6H2O (41.0 mg) were completely mixed with deionized water (20 mL) until fully dissolved, for preparing a homogenous solution with stirring assistance. After hydrothermal at 160 °C for 10 h, the bulk aggregate was then quickly frozen and freeze dried with vacuum to obtain Ni-CDs. Afterward, the Ni-CDs underwent pyrolysis at a temperature of 900 °C for a duration of 2 h in an N2 atmosphere, resulting in the formation of Ni-NC. The NC was achieved by pyrolysis of CDs which prepared in a similar way to Ni-CDs without adding NiCl2·6H2O.

Synthesis of NiPt-NC and Pt-NC: 50 mg of the as-prepared Ni-NC and 350 μL H2PtCl6·6H2O (20 mg mL- 1) were dispersed in 30 mL of deionized water, after stirring for 2 h, rapidly added 2 mL of freshly configured 0.1 M NaBH4 solution, and the reaction was terminated after 15 s. The NiPt-NC was obtained after filtration and vacuum drying at 60 °C. Under the same conditions as in the above synthetic procedure, replacing Ni-NC with NC obtains Pt-NC.

The NiPt-NC is prepared via a facile route as illustrated in Fig. 1 a, Specifically, we initiate the synthesis by fabricating nickel-doped amino carbon dots (Ni-CDs) by using renewable and non-toxic chitosan as the precursor, a process that leverages the complexation reaction between Ni2+ and amine groups. After the subsequent pyrolysis, the Ni-CDs interconnect with each other and self-assemble into nanosheet structure with atomically dispersed Ni-NC. The absence of substantial structural transformations during pyrolysis ensures that the Ni-CDs maintain a stable and well-spaced arrangement of Ni atoms, thereby preventing their aggregation [21]. Finally, the Pt NPs were deposited on the Ni-NC to generate NiPt-NC with well-dispersed Pt NPs. As a control, NC and Pt-NC samples were prepared in a similar way to Ni-NC and NiPt-NC, respectively, without introducing Ni precursor. It is noteworthy that throughout the entire synthesis process, precursors that are abundant in source and biodegradable are selected, and the reaction is carried out under mild conditions, avoiding the use of harsh chemicals and high-energy-consuming operation steps. This is conducive to energy conservation and environmental friendliness.

The CDs and Ni-CDs exhibit the inherent optical properties of carbon dots, which is as evidenced by ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) absorption spectra and photoluminescence (PL) spectra (Fig. S1). Moreover, the results of X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), and Raman characterizations, as shown in Fig. S2, indicate the successful synthesis of CDs and Ni-CDs with an average size of about 4 nm and a certain degree of graphitization. The Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) in Fig. 1b discloses the characteristic peaks corresponding to functional groups, including amino (-NH2), hydroxyl (-OH), and carbonyl (CO) on the surfaces of the CDs and Ni-CDs. The O and N in these functional groups are beneficial for coordinating and coupling with Ni2+, forming a stable CDs-Ni2+ structure [22]. This is corroborated by the intensified and blue-shifted absorption peak of O-H/N-H (at 3430 cm- 1) in Ni-CDs compared to CDs, which is indicative of the chemical interaction between Ni2+ and the CDs [23]. After pyrolysis, the majority of oxygen-containing functional groups were eliminated, and the resultant NC and Ni-NC display CN and C-N stretching vibrations, confirming the successful incorporation of N atoms into the carbon support. The XRD patterns presented in Fig. 1c display a distinct diffraction peak at 26.4°, corresponding to the (002) crystalline plane of the CDs. This observation confirms the retention of the CDs structure in the prepared samples [21]. For Pt-NC and NiPt-NC, the weak diffraction peaks at 40° and 46° denote the Pt (111) and Pt (200) crystal planes [24], respectively. Of note, the diffraction peaks associated with Ni species were not detected in both Ni-NC and NiPt-NC, indicating that Ni is highly dispersed in the samples [25]. The Raman spectra depicted in Fig. 1d reveal two prominent peaks at approximately 1350 and 1580 cm- 1, which correspond to the D band and G band, respectively. The ID1/IG values of NC, Ni-NC, Pt-NC, and NiPt-NC are 1.55, 1.43, 1.41, and 1.36, respectively. The ID1/IG ratios in these samples are all greater than 1, indicating that they contain abundant defect structures, which would generates additional active sites for ORR and HER [26,27]. Additionally, the lowest ID1/IG ratio of 1.36 observed for NiPt-NC indicates that the co-introduction of Pt and Ni enhances the graphitization degree, therefore augmenting the conductivity and electrocatalytic activity of the catalyst [28,29]. Moreover, the NiPt-NC manifests the largest BET surface area and micropore/mesoporous volume (Fig. 2 and Table S2), which would accelerate mass transfer process and improve the accessibility of active centers [30].

The morphology and particle size distribution of the catalysts can be discerned through the HR-TEM images. Both NC and Ni-NC exhibit nanosheet morphology with a size of 20 nm (Fig. 3 a and b), which is formed by the self-assembly of adjacent CDs via intermolecular hydrogen bonding during the pyrolysis process. Meanwhile, no Ni-related particles present in the HR-TEM and high-angle annular dark-field (HAADF) images of Ni-NC (Fig. S3), indicating that Ni is distributed at the atomic level. In addition, the Pt NPs on the surfaces of samples Pt-NC and NiPt-NC are uniformly dispersed, with sizes of 2.12 nm and 1.96 nm, respectively (Fig. 3c and d), suggesting that both NC and Ni-NC could supply plenty of anchor sites to promote Pt dispersion. In the magnifying image of NiPt-NC (inset in Fig. 3d), the lattice spacings of 0.226 nm and 0.196 nm correspond to the (111) and (200) planes of Pt, respectively [24]. The aberration-corrected HAADF scanning transmission electron microscopy STEM (AC-HAADF-STEM) allows for the observation of numerous compact bright spots, which correspond to the enhanced visibility of Pt crystals attributed to their higher atomic numbers and the exposure of specific crystal facets [10] (Fig. 3e). Meanwhile, from the corresponding elemental mapping presented in Fig. 3f-h, the observation reveals that N is uniformly doped in the carbon sheets. The Ni element is homogeneously distributed across the substrate, with no Ni-based aggregates present, while the majority of Pt elements are distributed in the shape of spherical particles. The Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) line-scanning and electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS) analyses were further conducted to further verify the distribution status of Pt and Ni. The line-scanning profiles clearly demonstrated a uniform distribution of Pt and Ni at individual particle positon, as indicated by the red dashed circle in Fig. 3e. The high-intensity signal of Pt in Fig. 3i and corresponding EDS analysis result (Fig. S4) confirm the elevated concentration of Pt at these locations [31,32]. Concurrently, EELS revealed that the isolated Ni single-atom co-exist with N at the atomic level, forming a Ni-Nx coordination structure in this region (Fig. 3j and k). These findings indicating Ni-Nx and Pt are closely located for a possible synergistic effect to promote the catalytic performance of NiPt-NC. Furthermore, there are still plenty of independent Ni-Nx sites at the atomic scale in the NiPt-NC, as shown in the blue circle area in Fig. 3e and l. These sites may serve as supplementary active centers, conferring additional catalytic activity to the material.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted to investigate the electronic structure of the prepared catalysts. The XPS survey spectra corroborate the presence of C, N, O, and Pt, as depicted in Fig. S5. Duo to the fact that the content of Ni in Ni-NC and NiPt-NC is low and less than 0.3 wt% (Table S2), the Ni-related peaks are not obvious. The N 1s spectra can be deconvoluted into three distinct peaks as shown in Fig. 4 a, which correspond to pyridine-N (ca. 398.2 eV), graphitic-N (ca. 401.0 eV), and oxidized-N (ca. 403.3 eV). All catalysts demonstrated abundant pyridinic N and graphitic N, which have been evidenced to enhance the ORR performance [33,34]. Of note, the pyridine-N peak of Ni-NC is noticeably shifted to higher binding energy compared to that of NC, indicating the formation of a Ni-Nx bond in Ni-NC [35]. The C 1s spectra of NC and Ni-NC are similar (Fig. S6), and lack features indicative of C-Ni bonding, which suggests that the carbon atoms do not bond with Ni [36,37]. The Ni 2p spectra in Fig. 4b show no evidence of zero-valent Ni species in either Ni-NC or NiPt-NC, confirming that Ni atoms are predominantly coordinated with surrounding nitrogen atoms [38]. As detailed in Table S4, the nitrogen content in Ni-NC and NiPt-NC is 5.60% and 6.93%, respectively, and the high nitrogen content may favorable for the formation of Ni-N bonds and the enhancement of nitrogen coordination number [39]. The electron transfer from Ni to the Pt is verified by a positive peak-shift of Ni 2p in the NiPt-NC compared to Ni-NC, along with the negative shift of Pt 4f of NiPt-NC in comparison to Pt-NC, as illustrated in Fig. 4b and c [40]. This would lead to an accumulation of electrons at the Pt center and modulate the d-band center of it, and thus optimizing the adsorption energy of intermediate species (∗OH and ∗H) and enhancing the catalytic activity of Pt for both ORR and HER [6,41]. X-ray absorption fine structure (XAFS) spectroscopy was further used to investigate the valency and local coordination geometry of the Ni and Pt atoms. The Ni K-edge X-ray absorption near-edge structure (XANES) spectra (Fig. 4d) indicated that the adsorption threshold position of Ni-NC and NiPt-NC situates between that of NiO and Ni foil, suggesting that Ni is present in an oxidized state. The calculated valence states of Ni in these samples were 1.60 and 1.62, respectively, aligning with the XPS analysis. Meanwhile, the positive energy shift observed in the NiPt-NC spectrum relative to the Ni-NC spectrum suggests an elevated valence state of Ni following Pt incorporation, potentially due to the electron transfer from Ni to Pt. Examination of the Ni K-edge Fourier transform-extended XAFS (FT-EXAFS) spectrum provides additional insights into bonding characteristics. The FT-EXAFS spectra at the Ni K-edge (Fig. 4e) exhibits a single prominent peak at approximately 1.4 Å in both the Ni-NC and NiPt-NC, which is indicative of a probable Ni-O/N interaction. The absence of the Ni-Ni bond at around 2.2 Å indicates that no formation of Ni aggregates and Ni exists in a single atom state [42]. The wavelet-transform EXAFS (WT-EXAFS) spectra offer further insight into the coordination characteristics. In the case of Ni-NC and NiPt-NC, the WT-EXAFS spectra (Fig. 4f) do not exhibit Ni-Ni signal, which is distinct from the Ni-Ni backscattering path observed at approximately 8.2 Å- 1 in both Ni foil and NiO. Instead, a signal appears at approximately 5.6 Å- 1, which belongs to Ni-N coordination, since it is lower than the Ni-O coordination signal at 6.3 Å- 1. Analysis of the EXAFS fitting (Fig. S7; Table S5) reveals that the average N coordination numbers of Ni atom in Ni-NC and NiPt-NC are 4.8 and 5.2, respectively, suggesting that Ni exists in the form of Ni-N5 at the atomic level in carbon substrate. Interestingly, compared to Ni-NC, the Ni-N bond length in NiPt-NC decreases from 1.886 Å to 1.827 Å, implying the possible interaction between Pt and Ni-NC strengthens the Ni-N bonding. In the Pt L3-edge XANES spectra as illustrated in Fig. 4g, the white line intensities of Pt-NC and NiPt-NC are intermediate between those of Pt foil and PtO2, indicating a partial oxidation state of the Pt within the samples. The white line intensity is directly proportional to the unoccupied 5d orbitals, and these unoccupied 5d electronic states primarily attributed to hybridization between Pt and light atoms (C/N/O) in the support matrix [43,44]. NiPt-NC exhibits a lower white line intensity compared to Pt-NC, suggesting a reduced oxidation state of Pt and an evolution in the electronic structure, likely due to the presence of Ni. The Fourier-transformed (FT) k3-weighted Pt L3-edge EXAFS spectra of Pt-NC and NiPt-NC display two principal peaks at around 1.8 Å and 2.4 Å, respectively, assigning to Pt–N/C and Pt-Pt coordination, as shown in Fig. 4h. Additionally, the peak at 2.3 Å is indicative of the formation of Pt-Ni bonds in NiPt-NC. WT analysis further corroborates these findings (Fig. 4i). Specifically, the signals at about 5 Å, 7.5 Å, and 11 Å for NiPt-NC are assignable to Pt-N/C, Pt–Ni, and Pt-Pt coordination, respectively [45]. The results presented herein validate the bonding of Pt with Ni-NC, implying the electronic interaction between them.

The ORR performance of the prepared catalysts and Pt/C was evaluated in a three-electrode system. Fig. 5 a and Table S6 shows a comparison of the linear scanning voltammetry (LSV) curves in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH. The half-wave potential (E1/2) for ORR are ranked as follows: NiPt-NC ≈ Pt/C > Pt-NC > Ni-NC > NC. From the slightly increased E1/2 of Ni-NC (0.67 V) in comparison to NC (0.64 V), it can be concluded that the catalytic activity Ni-N5 sites in Ni-NC for ORR is average. Of note, the ORR performances of NiPt-NC and Pt-NC are significantly superior to Ni-NC, indicating by the apparent increased E1/2. Meanwhile, the ORR activity of NiPt-NC has been further improved compared to Pt-NC. These results demonstrate that Pt sites dominantly contribute to the ORR in NiPt-NC, and the electronic interactions between Pt and Ni-N5 site would boost the ORR behavior of NiPt-NC. As shown in Fig. 5b, NiPt-NC presents the highest mass activity of 0.11 A mgPt -1 at the potential of 0.9 V, which is 2.0 and 4.2 times that of Pt-NC and Pt/C, respectively. Furthermore, NiPt-NC possesses a Tafel slope of 73.3 mV dec- 1 (Fig. 5c), which was lower than that of the other control catalysts, indicating that NiPt-NC possessed faster ORR kinetics. To further investigate the reaction mechanism of the NiPt-NC in more detail, LSV tests were carried out at different rotational speeds ranging from 400 to 2025 rpm (Fig. 5d and Fig. S8). The Koutecky-Levich (K-L) plots of NiPt-NC showed the average electron transfer number was 3.94 (inset in Fig. 5d), consistent with the result of Rotating Ring-Disk Electrode (RRDE) test, which showthat NiPt-NC had an HO2 --yield of 13 % and an electron transfer number of 3.8 (Fig. 5e). The decent stability of the NiPt-NC was verified by the accelerated stability tests (ASTs), as shown in Fig. 5f, the slightly decay of 15 mV in E1/2 is found after 5000 continuous CV cycles. We conducted XRD characterization of the post-reaction catalyst material (Fig. S9). The results show that the state of the Pt species remains unchanged and no new diffraction peaks appear, which means that the Pt nanoparticles maintain their crystalline structure during the catalytic process without significant dissolution, aggregation or transformation. The comparison of the performance and stability between NiPt-NC and the currently reported catalysts is tabulated in Table S7, which further attests to its excellent catalytic performance.

Motivated by its promising performance in the ORR, the viability of NiPt-NC in RZABs was assessed, as depicted in Fig. 6 a. For comparative purposes, an assembly featuring Pt/C+RuO2 as the air cathode was employed under identical experimental conditions. In Fig. 6b, RZABs with the NiPt-NC+RuO2 cathode exhibit an open-circuit potential of 1.43 V equal to that assembled with Pt/C+RuO2. Moreover, the NiPt-NC+RuO2-based RZABs achieved a peak power density of 176 mW cm- 2, surpassing the performance of Pt/C+RuO2-based RZABs (Fig. 6c). The rate capability analysis is presented in Fig. 6d, as the current density incrementally increases from 1 to 50 mA cm- 2, the RZABs with NiPt-NC exhibit superior discharge rate capacity and stability in comparison to the RZABs assembled by Pt/C. Additionally, from Fig. 6e we can see that NiPt-NC+RuO2-based RZABs maintained a stable discharge voltage of approximately 1.26 V and a specific capacity of 747 mAh gZn
-
1 at 10 mA cm- 2. In contrast, Pt/C+RuO2-based RZABs demonstrates a lower discharging voltage (1.22 V) and specific capacity of 722 mAh gZn - 1. To further ascertain the stability and reliability of the NiPt-NC+RuO2-based RZABs, a long-term cycling test was conducted. After a 960-cycle test over a period of 160 h at a current density of 10 mA cm- 2, the discharge voltage for the NiPt-NC+RuO2-based RZABs varied minimally, shifting from 1.25 to 1.18 V, and the charge voltage changed from 1.96 to 2.02 V (Fig. 6f), indicating excellent cycling performance. Notably, the round-trip efficiency of RZABs using the NiPt-NC+RuO2 air cathode decreases by only 5.3% and has a charge-discharge voltage gap of 0.84 V after 960 cycles, In contrast, the round-trip efficiency of the Pt/C+RuO2-based RZABs experiences a reduction of 6.5% after 300 cycles (which is approximately 50 h). As evidenced by the results above, the NiPt-NC+RuO2-based RZABs demonstrates exceptional long-term cycle stability and recharging capability, suggesting its application prospects for RZABs.

The HER performance of the prepared catalysts in 1.0 M KOH is shown in Fig. 7 a-c. From the LSV curves in Fig. 7a, it can be seen that the original NC support shows negligible alkaline HER activity (546 mV) at 10 mA cm- 2, while the overpotential is significantly reduced with the introduction of Ni-N5 sites in Ni-NC (310 mV). After introducing Pt species, the resulting NiPt-NC shows a near-zero onset potential and a low overpotential of 48 mV, which is smaller than that of Pt-NC (96 mV) and commercial Pt/C (50 mV), indicating the key role of Ni-N5 sites and Pt species in promoting the HER process. This may be due to the introduction of Ni-N5 sites with strong oxygen affinity, promoting H-OH bond breaking and promoting ∗H intermediates to overflow to metal Pt sites to generate H2. Then the Tafel slope of the polarization curve is used to evaluate the catalytic kinetics. For NiPt-NC, the calculated Tafel slope is 50.3 mV dec- 1, which is much lower than that of Pt-NC (78.7 mV dec- 1) and Ni-NC (115.2 mV dec- 1), reflecting the fastest HER kinetic process for NiPt-NC (Fig. 7b). And the Tafel slope value is between 40 mV dec- 1 and 120 mV dec- 1 revealed the HER process on NiPt-NC predominantly undergoes the Volmer-Heyrovsky mechanism [46]. In addition, NiPt-NC has the mass activities of 0.67 A mgPt - 1 and 1.69 A mgPt - 1 at 20 and 50 mV, respectively, which are 19.7 and 10.0 times that of Pt/C. Remarkably, the HER performance of NiPt-NC outperforms most of the Pt-based electrocatalysts studied in recent studies (Table S8). Cyclic voltammetry tests were additionally conducted at various sweep rates, to obtain the double layer capacity values for evaluating the electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) (Fig. S10). Fig. 7d illustrates that NiPt-NC pocesses the highest Cdl value of 7.05 mF cm- 2 among the catalysts synthesized, which might be originated from the synergetic interrelation between the Pt NPs and Ni-NC. Furthermore, we assembled a two-electrode electrolyzer employing Pt/C||RuO2 and NiPt-NC||RuO2 catalysts, respectively, to compare and evaluate the practical application potential of NiPt-NC for water splitting. The NiPt-NC||RuO2-based apparatus only requires a potential of 1.50 V to achieve a current density of 10 mA cm- 2, which is on par with the Pt/C||RuO2-based one (Fig. 7e). In addition, the NiPt-NC catalyst demonstrates exceptional stability in the HER, as evidenced by the negligible decay in current density over a 30-h period at a voltage of 1.56 V (Fig. 7f). This performance indicates the potential applicability of the NiPt-NC catalyst in practical device applications.

Further free energy calculation were conducted to elucidate the reaction mechanisms underlying the ORR and HER on the Pt-NC and NiPt-NC. Based on the results obtained from XRD, XPS and EXAFS, a computational model was developed comprising a 13-atom cuboctahedral Pt13 cluster and a graphene layer containing Ni-N5 site to simulate NiPt-NC structure. For comparative purposes, a corresponding Pt-NC model with an N4 site was also constructed. The optimized structural models are presented in Fig. S11. The reaction intermediates involved in ORR encompass OOH∗, O∗, and ∗OH (Fig. 8 a and c). As depicted in Fig. 8a, the ORR free energy diagram illustrates that at a standard electrode potential of 1.23 V, the ∗OOH intermediate in the two model catalysts exhibits a preference for a path of O–O bond cleavage into O∗ (4e- process) rather than a HO2 - formation (2e- process). Furthermore, it has been noted that the fourth step of ∗OH + e-→OH- presents the largest difference in free energy, indicating that the desorption of ∗OH from Pt sites serves as the rate-determining step (RDS). In comparison to Pt-NC, NiPt-NC demonstrates the lower RDS energy barrier of 0.82 eV, signifying its thermodynamic favorability and easier desorption of ∗OH to release active sites for subsequent reactions. The above results indicate Ni-N5 site is more advantageous than N4 sites to enhance the intrinsic activity of Pt for ORR.

The Pt-group metals generally exhibit limited catalytic activity in the alkaline HER, primarily limited by the inefficient adsorption/dissociation process of water, known as the Volmer step (∗OH + H2O + e- ⇆∗H + OH-), which is genarally recongnized to be related to the tardness of ∗OH desorption [47,48]. Consequently, the computational analysis of the adsorption/desorption properties of H2O molecules and the intermediates was undertaken. From Fig. 8b we can find that the RDS for Pt-NC is associated with water adsorption, indicating by the high free energy of 0.26 eV. While the Volmer step for water adsorption/dissociation is spontaneously on NiPt-NC with a free energy of only -0.03 eV, which guarantee the rapid proton supply for H2 formation. Meanwhile, both Pt-NC and NiPt-NC exhibit relatively small free energy barriers during the conversion of ∗H to H2, with that of NiPt-NC being merely 0.06 eV, indicating the rapid kinetics of it for HER [49]. These results imply that the incorporation of Ni-N5 sites in the support considerably augments the water dissociation capacity of Pt sites, substantially decreases the water dissociation energy barrier, and expedites the Volmer step. As shown in Fig. S12, further validation is provided by the challenging water dissociation step on the catalyst when Pt is loaded onto single-atom carbon support with classic M-N4 coordination (Ni-N4C). The more electron transfer from Ni-N5 sites to Pt and the stronger interaction effectively modulate the adsorption strength of reaction intermediates, facilitating the occurrence of the reaction. The HER mechanism for alkaline environment on NiPt-NC is shown in Fig. 8d. Charge density difference and density of states (DOS) calculations have provided further insights into the intrinsic catalytic activity of NiPt-NC. It is evident that the charge rearrangement occurred on NiPt-NC(Fig. 8e) in comparison to Pt-NC, indicating by the electrons transferring from Ni-N5 sites to neighboring Pt sites and the associated increase in charge accumulation at the Pt sites. Furthermore, the net charges (N e) are -0.01 e and -0.12 e for Pt in Pt-NC and NiPt-NC, respectively, the elevated charge at Pt sites in NiPt-NC suggests an enhanced MSI in this sample. This observation is consistent with the results of XPS analysis. More importantly, this also has significant implications for the stability of the catalyst. The electron-rich Pt species on the surface is conducive to slowing down the formation of Pt oxides at high potentials. Meanwhile, the strong interaction between Pt particles and the substrate helps prevent the coalescence and detachment of Pt from the support during cycling. The total DOS depicted in Fig. S13 uncovers elevated electron states at the Fermi level for Pt-NC and NiPt-NC models, signifying the decent conductivity of them. Additionally, the projected DOS (PDOS) for NiPt-NC in Fig. 8f exhibits a pronounced orbital overlap of Pt 5d and Ni 3d around the Fermi level, indicative of intensified d-d orbital coupling and charge transfer interactions between Pt and Ni atoms, therefore presenting a decreased d-band center of -2.18 eV and attenuating the adsorption energy of ∗OH intermediates [50]. This is further supported by the minimal orbital overlap between Pt 5d and O 2p from adsorbed ∗ OH intermediates on NiPt-NC (Fig. S14). The above theoretical calculation results supply the powerful evidence for that Ni-N5 sites in the carbon support could efficiently regulate the electronic structure of Pt, and optimize the adsorption capability of intermediates, such as ∗H and ∗ OH et al. to boost both ORRand HER behaviors.

In summary, we provide an effective tactic to design high-performance low-Pt electrocatalyst by integrating the highly dispersed Pt NPs and atomically dispersed Ni-N5 site-rich carbon dots substrate. The strong interaction between Pt and Ni-N5 sites effectively optimized the adsorption of key intermediates for both ORR and HER, thus significantly enhancing catalyst activity and stability. In particular, the NiPt-NC has achieved significantly low overpotentials of 48 mV to sustain 10 mA cm- 2 in HER under alkaline conditions, as well as a high E1/2 of 0.83 V in ORR. Furthermore, a decent practical performance, including a power density of 176 mW cm- 2, a specific capacity of 747 mAh gZn- 1 at 10 mA cm- 2, and a outstanding long-term stability with only 5.3 % decay of the round-trip efficiency after 960 cycles (160 h), was achieved in the RZABs test. In addition, the overall water splitting in the NiPt-NC-based two-electrode water electrolyzer exhibits a current density of 10 mA cm- 2 in 1 M KOH with only a potential of 1.50 V and remains almost constant current density of 20 mA cm- 2 for 30 h. This study offers an efficient approach for producing high-performance and cost-effective Pt-based bifunctional catalyst for electrochemical energy storage.

Yuemei Liu: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Junhong Ma: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Shaorui Jia: Software, Data curation. Chaoyun Ma: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Peiran Wu: Software. Lijing Yang: Visualization, Data curation. Liuliu Shen: Software, Resources. Guirong Zhang: Resources.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relatio nships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Grant No. 21862021]; and the Shanghai cooperation organization science and technology partnership project [Grant No.2021E01009].

The authors thank the staff at BL17B1beamline of the National Facility for Protein Science in Shanghai (NFPS), Shanghai Advanced Research Institute, CAS, for providing technical support in XAFS data collection and analysis.
